findings.title: SSH exposed to public internet on port 22
findings.detail: SSH daemon is listening on 100.67.83.108:22 and accepting connections from any IP. While SSH key authentication is configured, internet-facing SSH increases attack surface and risk of brute force or zero-day exploits.
findings.remediation: Restrict SSH to specific IPs via firewall (ufw allow from <trusted_ip> to any port 22) or use fail2ban. Consider moving SSH to a non-standard port.

findings.title: No firewall detected
findings.detail: Both iptables and ufw are unavailable or inactive. Host has multiple services exposed to the internet (ports 22, 80, 443, 443/udp) with no network-level filtering.
findings.remediation: Enable and configure ufw: ufw default deny incoming; ufw allow 22/tcp; ufw allow 80/tcp; ufw allow 443/tcp; ufw enable

findings.title: HTTP (port 80) exposed to public internet
findings.detail: HTTP server listening on *:80 - accessible from any IP. Verify this is intentional web hosting.
findings.remediation: If not intentional, stop the web server. If intentional, ensure software is kept updated and consider restricting to specific IPs via firewall.

findings.title: HTTPS (port 443) exposed to public internet
findings.detail: HTTPS server listening on *:443 - accessible from any IP. Ensure TLS is properly configured and software is up to date.
findings.remediation: If not intentional, stop the web server. Ensure TLS configuration is secure (disable TLS 1.0/1.1, use modern ciphers).

findings.title: Unknown service on port 51567
findings.detail: TCP service listening on 100.67.83.108:51567 with no immediate identification. Process association could not be determined from ss output alone.
findings.remediation: Identify the process: ss -tlnp | grep 51567 or lsof -i :51567. Close if unnecessary.

findings.title: UDP port 443 exposed to public internet
findings.detail: UDP service listening on *:443 - could be used for amplification attacks or is experimental QUIC/H3 traffic.
findings.remediation: Verify this is intentional. If not needed, disable the service exposing this port.

findings.title: mDNS/LLMNR (port 5355) bound to 0.0.0.0
findings.detail: Service listening on 0.0.0.0:5355 (and :::5355) - this is mDNS/LLMNR responder. While local to the host network, binding to 0.0.0.0 could enable network-based attacks.
findings.remediation: If not needed, disable avahi-daemon. Otherwise, acceptable for a host-wide discovery service.

findings.title: SSH known_hosts has overly permissive permissions
findings.detail: known_hosts file has permissions 644 (rw-r--r--) instead of 600 (rw-------). This allows other users to read the file.

findings.title: No authorized_keys file present
findings.detail: No ~/.ssh/authorized_keys file exists. SSH is configured for key-only auth but there are no authorized keys configured - this could lock the user out if password auth is also disabled.
findings.remediation: Ensure SSH keys are properly configured or password auth is intentionally disabled with a fallback access method.

findings.title: OpenClaw gateway service not running
findings.detail: Gateway is in 'activating' state with last exit code 1. The service is configured but not functioning. However, it does bind to loopback only (127.0.0.1:18789), which is secure.
findings.remediation: Run 'openclaw gateway start' or check logs: journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway.service -n 50
